content.full_text:
Ross Johnson
Assembly Republican Leader
Doris Allen
William Baker
Carol Bentley
Paula Boland
James Brulte
Chris Chandler
Gerald Felando
Gil Ferguson
Bill Filante
Robert Frazee
Nolan Frizzelle
Bev Hansen
Trece Harvey
Paul Horcher

California Legislature

Tricia Hunter
Bill Jones
David Kelley
David Knowles
Bill Lancaster
Tim Leslie
John Lewis
Tom Mays
Tom McClintock
Richard Mountjoy
Pat Nolan
Charles Quackenbush
Andrea Seastrand
Stan Statham
Paul Woodruff
Cathie Wright
Phil Wyman

[SEAL: STATE OF CALIFORNIA]

ASSEMBLY REPUBLICAN CAUCUS

January 5, 1991

Mr. John Wallace
F.P.P.C. Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Pursuant to our conversation of 11/8/90, I would like to request
advice with regard to the formation of a legislative task force
titled the Conference on the Preservation of the Family and an
associated legislative project titled Family Congress.

By way of background, please recall we discussed the following:

In August of 1988, a group of legislators led by Assemblyman Tim
Leslie and Senator Newton Russell formed an ad-hoc Task Force
called the Republican Family Task Force -- a group of 22
legislators who each had an interest in researching the effects of
government policy on families in California.

The following September the Task Force held four public hearings
throughout the state to solicit public input on the role of
government vs. the family. Then, based on testimony received at
the hearings, the members of the Task Force introduced a 35 bill
legislative package dealing with "family issues."

In 1990, the Task Force was renamed the Conference on the
Preservation of the Family to more accurately reflect the goals of
the group. A decision was made by each of the members to further
their work by holding a state-wide public conference which they
have titled the Family Congress. To help fund the overhead
operation costs of the Family Congress (ie. publicity, speaker
coordination, etc.), each member would like to contribute some of
his/her office-holder expense funds or surplus campaign funds into
a joint bank account.

(916) 445-0365        1100 J Street - Sacramento, CA 95814        (916) 445-3260

Printed on Recycled Paper

Mr. John Wallace
F.P.P.C. Legal Division
Page 2


This raises the following questions which require your attention:

1. Would use of legislator's surplus campaign funds to help pay
for the costs of a public/legislative conference be deemed
acceptable under the new personal use law (SB 1431, Chap. 1452,
Stats. 1989) and current Government Code in that the conference
would be "reasonably related to a governmental purpose?"

2. Proposition 73 banned the transfer of campaign funds from one
candidate or candidate-controlled committee to "any other
candidate's campaign bank account, candidate, candidate's
controlled committee, file, or any of the candidate's other
controlled committees." (Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18534)

According to FPPC guidelines and Government Code, a controlled
committee is any "person or combination of persons which" apart
from making independent expenditures or campaign contributions,
"receives contributions totaling more than $1,000 or more in a
calendar year." Contribution is defined as "A payment...unless
it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not
made for political purposes" (emphasis added). (1990 Campaign
Disclosure Information Manual A)

An October 26th FPPC memorandum regarding Proposed Regulation
18587 stated that the general rule of the personal use law "is
that any expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum,
reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental
purpose associated with the candidate's office." This
definition lists three separate criteria for expenditures:
political, legislative and governmental.

Regulation 18217, regarding nonprofit organizations as
controlled committees states that an organization is operated
for political purposes if either of the following applies: (1)
The organization receives or expends funds for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters for
or against the nomination or election of a candidate or the
qualification or passage of any measure; or (2) The
organization makes contributions to candidates or their
controlled committees.

The charter of the Family Congress and the Conference on the
Preservation of the Family is to directly create legislation to
be introduced in the next legislative session and is not to
support or oppose any candidate for office. In addition, both

Mr. John Wallace
F.P.P.C. Legal Division
Page 3

groups are controlled by all 23 members of the task force
(Legislators) with the input of 100+ "public advisory committee
members."

Given these circumstances,

a)  What distinguishes the three areas of the personal use law
(governmental purpose, legislative purpose and political
purpose) as applied to the personal use law, the definition
of "contributions," and the definition of political
purposes set forth in Regulation 18217? How is each
purpose defined?

b)  From the above definitions, does the work of the Family
Congress and the Conference on the Preservation of the
Family fit the "governmental and/or legislative purpose"
associated with the candidate's office as defined in the
personal use law or does it fit the "political purposes"
listed in the personal use law and/or Regulation 18217?

c)  Would the establishment of a joint bank account for the
purposes of operating the Family Congress be permissible
under current statutes given that no contributions or
expenditures would be made to support or oppose candidates
and/or ballot measures?

1)  Can the bank account be set up so that one or two
legislators have authority to sign checks and make
payments?

2)  Can the bank account be set up so that one or two
legislative staff members, under the direction of a
legislative body such as the Assembly Republican
Caucus, have the authority to sign checks and make
payments?

d)  Would such a joint bank account violate the current Prop.
73-imposed transfer ban of campaign funds, and could
legislators contribute to this bank account from their
surplus campaign funds? their current campaign
funds? their office expense funds?

Mr. John Wallace
F.P.P.C. Legal Division
Page 4


3.  Proposition 73 limited mass mailings at public expense and set
specific guidelines for such mailings.  Under current limits
and rules:

a)  Can the task force print letterhead listing the name of the
chairman of the group (a Legislator) and the chairman's
capitol office phone number if the printing and mailing
costs are paid for by the joint bank account (not public
funds)?  Can the letterhead include the names of all of the
Legislative members of the task force?

b)  What limits does the Political Reform Act and Prop. 73 set
on such mailings paid for by a group such as the Conference
on the Preservation of the Family?

I would appreciate your prompt response.  If you have any questions
regarding this request, please contact me at (916) 445-3260.

Sincerely,

Jeff Evans
Senior Consultant,
Assembly Republican Caucus
